item.title: “Suppose, monks, a man is struck by a single arrow. They do not strike him with a second arrow. Thus, monks, that man experiences the…
item.description:
“Suppose, monks, a man is struck by a single arrow. They do not strike him with a second arrow. Thus, monks, that man experiences the feeling of one arrow. So too, monks, a well-taught noble disciple, when touched by a painful feeling, does not sorrow, grieve, lament, beat his breast, or become distraught. He experiences one feeling — a bodily feeling, not a mental feeling. Touched by that painful feeling, he does not feel aversion. Because he does not feel aversion toward that painful feeling, the underlying tendency to aversion toward painful feeling does not persist. Touched by that painful feeling, he does not delight in sensual pleasures. Why? Because, monks, the well-taught noble disciple knows an escape from painful feeling other than sensual pleasure. Not delighting in sensual pleasure, the underlying tendency to greed for pleasant feeling does not persist. He knows as it actually is the origination, the cessation, the gratification, the peril, and the escape regarding these feelings. Because he knows … the underlying tendency to ignorance regarding neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling does not persist. If he feels a pleasant feeling, he feels it as one who is not bound. If he feels a painful feeling, he feels it as one who is not bound. If he feels a neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling, he feels it as one who is not bound. This, monks, is called a well-taught noble disciple who is not bound by birth, aging, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair. I say that he is not bound by suffering.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 36.6
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Householder, suppose near a village or town there is a forest grove. In it there is a tree full of ripe fruits, but none have…
item.description:
“Householder, suppose near a village or town there is a forest grove. In it there is a tree full of ripe fruits, but none have fallen to the ground. Then a man comes, wishing for fruit, seeking fruit, wandering here and there for fruit. He enters that forest, sees that tree full of ripe fruits, and thinks: ‘This tree is full of ripe fruits, but none have fallen. I know how to climb trees. Suppose I climb this tree, eat my fill, and fill my clothes.’ Then he climbs the tree, eats his fill, and fills his clothes. Then a second man comes, wishing for fruit, seeking fruit, wandering here and there for fruit, carrying a sharp axe. He enters that forest, sees that tree full of ripe fruits, and thinks: ‘This tree is full of ripe fruits, but none have fallen. I don’t know how to climb trees. Suppose I cut this tree down at the root, eat my fill, and fill my clothes.’ Then he cuts the tree down at the root. What do you think, householder? If the first man does not climb down quickly, when the tree falls, will it not break his hand, foot, or some part of his body, and will he not thereby meet death or deadly suffering?” “Yes, venerable sir.” “So too, householder, a noble disciple reflects: ‘The Blessed One has said that sensual pleasures are like a fruit tree — of much suffering, much despair, and the peril is even greater.’ Having seen this as it actually is with perfect wisdom … he cultivates such equanimity.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 54 – Potaliyasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Form is a lump of glowing ember, monks! Feeling is a lump of glowing ember! Perception is a lump of glowing ember! Formations are lumps…
item.description:
“Form is a lump of glowing ember, monks! Feeling is a lump of glowing ember! Perception is a lump of glowing ember! Formations are lumps of glowing ember! Consciousness is a lump of glowing ember! Seeing thus, monks, a well-taught noble disciple becomes disenchanted with form, disenchanted with feeling, disenchanted with perception, disenchanted with formations, disenchanted with consciousness. Being disenchanted, he becomes dispassionate. Through dispassion, he is liberated. In liberation, knowledge arises: ‘Liberated.’ He knows: ‘Birth is destroyed, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more of this state of being.’”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.136
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Venerable Kappa: “For those standing in the middle of the flood, In the terrifying current of existence, Overcome by old age and death — Tell…
item.description:
Venerable Kappa:
“For those standing in the middle of the flood,
In the terrifying current of existence,
Overcome by old age and death —
Tell me, sir, an island,
That there may be no further suffering.”

The Blessed One replied to Kappa:
“For those standing in the middle of the flood,
In the terrifying current of existence,
Overcome by old age and death —
I will tell you, Kappa, an island.

This island is unique —
Possessing nothing, not clinging —
I call it Nibbāna,
The destruction of old age and death.

Knowing this, mindful,
Attaining Nibbāna in this very life,
They do not come into Māra’s power,
Do not become servants of Māra.”

From: Sutta Nipāta 5.11
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Elder Parāpariya: The eye goes to forms — Without restraint, without checking, Not seeing the danger, That person does not escape suffering. The ear goes…
item.description:
Elder Parāpariya:

The eye goes to forms —
Without restraint, without checking,
Not seeing the danger,
That person does not escape suffering.

The ear goes to sounds —
Without restraint, without checking,
Not seeing the danger,
That person does not escape suffering.

Not seeing the escape,
Enjoying odors,
That person does not escape suffering,
Because of lust for smells.

Remembering sweet and sour as supreme,
Remembering bitter as supreme,
Enslaved by the taste of craving,
The mind cannot awaken.

Remembering contacts —
Beautiful and pleasurable —
Infatuated, conditioned by lust,
One meets many sufferings.

The mind toward all these things —
All five —
If unable to guard them,
Suffering follows that person.

From: Theragāthā 16.2
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, sensual pleasures are impermanent, empty, false, of a delusive nature. Monks, this is the mere greeting of a fool built upon deception. Sensual pleasures…
item.description:
“Monks, sensual pleasures are impermanent, empty, false, of a delusive nature. Monks, this is the mere greeting of a fool built upon deception. Sensual pleasures present and future, perceptions of sensual pleasure present and future — both belong to Māra’s realm, Māra’s domain, Māra’s bait, Māra’s food.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 106 – Āneñjasappāyasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Elder Nun Uppalavaṇṇā: “How tiresome are sensual desires! Impure, foul, full of thorns — Seeing the danger in sensual pleasures, Renunciation is firmly secure. I…
item.description:
Elder Nun Uppalavaṇṇā:

“How tiresome are sensual desires!
Impure, foul, full of thorns —
Seeing the danger in sensual pleasures,
Renunciation is firmly secure.
I went forth from Rājagaha,
Leaving home for homelessness.”

I know my former lives,
The divine eye is purified.
I know the minds of others,
The ear-element is purified.

I have attained psychic powers,
The destruction of defilements is attained.
I have attained the six direct knowledges —
The Buddha’s instruction is done.

With mind well subdued,
The bases of psychic power well developed,
The six direct knowledges are attained —
The Buddha’s instruction is done.

Sensual desires are like swords and spears —
They cut the aggregates to pieces.
What you call the pleasure of life —
For me, that sensual pleasure
Is no longer enticing.

Everywhere, delight is destroyed.
The mass of darkness,
The blinding darkness, has been shattered.
Know this, O Evil One —
You are Māra,
You are defeated.

From: Therīgāthā 11.1
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monk, ‘butcher’s knife’ is a synonym for the five strands of sensual pleasure; ‘lifting the butcher’s knife’ means abandoning these five strands of sensual pleasure.…
item.description:
“Monk, ‘butcher’s knife’ is a synonym for the five strands of sensual pleasure; ‘lifting the butcher’s knife’ means abandoning these five strands of sensual pleasure. ‘The wise man, taking the sword, dug up’ is the meaning. ‘Piece of meat’ is a synonym for delight and lust; ‘lifting the piece of meat’ means abandoning that delight and lust. ‘The wise man, taking the sword, dug up’ is the meaning. ‘Viper’ is a synonym for a monk who has destroyed the defilements; ‘let the viper be, do not touch the viper, pay homage to the viper’ is the meaning.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 23 – Vammikasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “By developing and cultivating the perception of impermanence, all lust for sensual pleasures is abandoned, all lust for form is abandoned, all lust for existence…
item.description:
“By developing and cultivating the perception of impermanence, all lust for sensual pleasures is abandoned, all lust for form is abandoned, all lust for existence is abandoned, all ignorance is abandoned, all conceit ‘I am’ is abandoned. Monks, just as a farmer in autumn, using a large plow, cuts through all the roots while plowing — so too, by developing and cultivating the perception of impermanence … all conceit ‘I am’ is abandoned. Monks, just as a grass-cutter cuts down grass, takes it by the top, shakes it up and down, left and right, and then throws it away — so too, by developing and cultivating the perception of impermanence … all conceit ‘I am’ is abandoned. Monks, just as when a bunch of mangoes is cut from its stalk, all the mangoes that were attached to the stalk are cut off with it — so too, by developing and cultivating the perception of impermanence … all conceit ‘I am’ is abandoned.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.102
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Venerable Todeyya: “One in whom desires do not dwell, One in whom craving is not found, One who has crossed beyond doubt — What is…
item.description:
Venerable Todeyya:
“One in whom desires do not dwell,
One in whom craving is not found,
One who has crossed beyond doubt —
What is the liberation of such a one?”

The Blessed One replied to Todeyya:
“One in whom desires do not dwell,
One in whom craving is not found,
One who has crossed beyond doubt —
There is no other kind of liberation.”

Venerable Todeyya:
“Does such a one have longing,
Or is he free from longing?
Does he have wisdom,
Or is he still acquiring wisdom?
O all-seeing sage, explain this to me,
So that I may know what a sage is, O Sakyan.”

The Blessed One:
“He is free from longing,
Without any longing —
He is wise, not acquiring wisdom.
Thus, Todeyya, know the sage:
Possessing nothing,
Not attached to sensual existence.”

From: Sutta Nipāta 5.10
📖 Read this sutta
